skill.name: experimental-data-analysis
skill.description: Statistical analysis and reporting for experimental datasets using t-tests and ANOVA with reproducible run-based execution.
skill.summary: Run statistical tests on experimental CSV data and generate reproducible reports with descriptive statistics, hypothesis tests, and effect sizes.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 32 static analysis findings evaluated as false positives. The scanner matched substrings such as 'DES' within 'Descriptive'/'Description'/'Design' (reported as weak cryptographic algorithm) and 'SAM' within the variable name 'SAMPLE_DATA' (reported as Windows SAM database). No actual cryptographic algorithms, credential access, or reconnaissance behavior is present. The skill is a legitimate experimental data analysis tool that runs documented Python scripts locally. External commands are limited to documented script execution in SKILL.md examples. The GitHub URL is a source attribution link. Risk level LOW: no confirmed security issues.

medium_findings.title: Documented Shell Command Execution in SKILL.md
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md contains Markdown code blocks showing bash commands to run Python scripts (init_run.py and analyze_experiment.py). This is instructional documentation for the legitimate skill workflow. No automated or uncontrolled execution. FALSE POSITIVE.

low_findings.title: Substring Match on 'DES' as Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Scanner flagged 'DES' in words like 'Descriptive', 'Description', and 'Design' as weak cryptographic algorithm usage. These are legitimate English words in statistical contexts. No DES or any encryption algorithm is used anywhere in the skill. FALSE POSITIVE.

low_findings.title: Substring Match on 'SAM' as Windows SAM Database
low_findings.description: Scanner flagged 'SAM' in variable name 'SAMPLE_DATA' as Windows Security Account Manager database access. SAMPLE_DATA is a Python list containing sample control/treatment group values for demonstration. No credential access. FALSE POSITIVE.

low_findings.title: Timestamp Formatting Flagged as System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Scanner flagged strftime usage for creating timestamped run directories as system reconnaissance. This is standard practice in reproducible data analysis workflows. No system enumeration intent. FALSE POSITIVE.

low_findings.title: GitHub Source URL Flagged as Hardcoded Network Endpoint
low_findings.description: Scanner flagged a GitHub repository URL at the top of SKILL.md as a hardcoded network endpoint. This is a source attribution link pointing to the skill's origin repository, not a data exfiltration target. FALSE POSITIVE.

low_findings.title: Heuristic Dangerous Combination (Aggregate of False Positives)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer aggregated documented code examples, source URL, and variable name substring matches into a heuristic 'dangerous combination' finding. Each individual finding is a false positive, so the aggregate is also a false positive. FALSE POSITIVE.

content.user_title: Run Statistical Tests on Experimental Data
content.value_statement: Transform experimental CSV data into reproducible statistical reports with automated t-tests and ANOVA. This skill guides users through data preparation, assumption validation, and inferential analysis to produce structured outputs ready for publication.
actual_capabilities: Load and prepare CSV experimental data for statistical analysis with configurable column mapping
Run independent and paired t-tests with automatic normality and variance checks
Perform one-way ANOVA for multi-group comparisons with configurable group and value columns
Generate descriptive statistics including count, mean, standard deviation, and standard error
Output structured analysis reports in both JSON and Markdown formats for easy review
Create timestamped run directories for traceability and experiment reproducibility
limitations: Requires CSV input with specific group column and value column format defined in configuration
Does not support non-parametric ANOVA alternatives such as Kruskal-Wallis
Cannot generate charts, plots, or visualizations for the analysis results
Limited to two-group comparisons (t-test) and one-way multi-group comparisons (ANOVA)

use_cases.title: Analyze Treatment vs Control Group Results
use_cases.description: A research scientist has CSV data from a drug trial with control and treatment groups. The skill runs a Welch t-test, computes descriptive statistics, and generates a reproducible report with p-values and effect sizes.

use_cases.title: Compare Multiple Experimental Conditions
use_cases.description: A lab technician measures cell growth across four different培养基 conditions. The skill runs one-way ANOVA to detect significant differences between groups and produces a formatted report.

use_cases.title: Generate Reproducible Analysis for Publications
use_cases.description: A graduate student needs statistically sound analysis for a research paper submission. The skill provides timestamped run directories, configurable analysis parameters, and standardized output format for auditability.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Two-Group Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a CSV file with control and treatment group data. Run a statistical analysis to compare the means and tell me if there is a significant difference.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has CSV data with two groups and wants a basic comparison

prompt_templates.title: ANOVA for Multiple Groups
prompt_templates.prompt: My experiment has three groups: low dose, medium dose, and control. Use ANOVA to compare all groups and identify which pairs show significant differences.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has three or more experimental groups requiring multi-group comparison

prompt_templates.title: Paired Before-After Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: I collected before and after measurements from the same 15 subjects. Configure the analysis for paired data and run a paired t-test with full statistical reporting.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has repeated measures or matched pairs data

prompt_templates.title: Custom Analysis with Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure the analysis to use my CSV file at data/experiment_results.csv with the response column named 'measurement' and the group column named 'condition'. Run a Welch t-test and save the output as a Markdown report.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user with custom file paths and column names

output_examples.input: Compare control and treatment groups for statistical significance.
output_examples.output: Descriptive statistics computed: Control group (n=15, mean=24.3, SD=3.1), Treatment group (n=15, mean=31.7, SD=3.5). Welch t-test result: t(27.8)=6.12, p<0.001. The treatment group mean is significantly higher than control. Effect size: Cohen's d=2.23. Assumption checks: normality passed, variance homogeneity passed.

output_examples.input: Run ANOVA on four dosage groups to find which ones differ.
output_examples.output: One-way ANOVA completed: F(3, 36)=8.74, p<0.001. Significant differences detected between groups. Post-hoc Tukey HSD results: High dose differs from Control (p<0.001) and Low dose (p=0.012). Medium dose differs from Control (p=0.008). No significant difference between High and Medium doses (p=0.234).

output_examples.input: Set up a new analysis run for my experiment data.
output_examples.output: Run directory created at outputs/runs/20260325_143022/. Configuration file initialized with default settings: input CSV set to data/experiment.csv, group column set to group, value column set to value. Sample data written for testing. Edit config.json to match your experiment before running analysis.

best_practices: Always run init_run.py first to create a clean run directory with proper configuration before analyzing new data
Verify your CSV file has the correct column names matching the configuration values for group_col and value_col
Review assumption check results for normality and variance homogeneity before interpreting test p-values
anti_patterns: Do not modify output files inside the run directory directly; recreate the run if you need to change analysis parameters
Do not use t-test configuration when you have three or more groups; switch to ANOVA for proper multi-group analysis
Do not skip data preparation steps for handling missing values and outliers before running inferential tests

faq.question: What file format does the input data need to be in?
faq.answer: The input data must be a CSV file with at least two columns: one for group labels and one for numeric measurement values.

faq.question: Can I analyze more than two groups with this skill?
faq.answer: Yes, set the test_type in the configuration to anova to compare three or more groups in a single analysis.

faq.question: Does this skill support paired data like before and after measurements?
faq.answer: Yes, set the paired option to true in the configuration file to run a paired t-test for matched or repeated measures.

faq.question: What output formats does the analysis produce?
faq.answer: The analysis generates both a JSON file with structured results and a Markdown report with summary tables and test statistics.

faq.question: Where are the analysis results saved on disk?
faq.answer: Results are saved in timestamped subdirectories under outputs/runs/ to keep each analysis run separate and traceable.

faq.question: Do I need to install any dependencies to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes, you need Python 3.10 or newer with the pandas, numpy, and scipy packages installed.
